## Reviewer's Guide Refactors SIMA fleet dispatch health-check logic into smaller helpers, snapshots concurrent collections to avoid enumeration overhead, and adds a dispatch tracking call to reduce cyclomatic complexity while preserving behavior and logging. #### Flow diagram for refactored SIMA fleet health check helpers ```mermaid flowchart TD A[ShouldSkipFleet_RunHealthCheck] A --> B[acct or acct.Positions null?] B -->|yes| Z[return] B -->|no| C[IsBrokerPositionFlat] C --> D[HasActiveFsmForAccount] D --> E[HasActivePositionForAccount] E --> F[dispatch pending via _dispatchSyncPendingExpKeys] F --> G[LogHealthCheckResult] subgraph Helpers C D E G end ``` ### File-Level Changes | Change | Details | Files | | ------ | ------- | ----- | | Refactor ShouldSkipFleet_RunHealthCheck into discrete helper methods to reduce cyclomatic complexity and improve readability while maintaining behavior. |
  • Replaced in-method broker position scan with IsBrokerPositionFlat helper that snapshots positions and searches by instrument name
  • Replaced inline FSM active-check loop with HasActiveFsmForAccount helper that snapshots _followerBrackets and iterates by account name
  • Replaced inline active position loop with HasActivePositionForAccount helper that snapshots activePositions and iterates by executing account name
  • Extracted the logging branch logic into LogHealthCheckResult helper, parameterized by booleans and account name
  • Updated main health-check method to compute booleans via helpers and delegate final logging to LogHealthCheckResult
| `src/V12_002.SIMA.Fleet.cs` | | Introduce dispatch tracking hook into fleet dispatch pump. |
  • Inserted TrackSimaDispatch invocation at the start of PumpFleetDispatch to track SIMA dispatch cycles
| `src/V12_002.SIMA.Fleet.cs` | ---
Tips and commands #### Interacting with Sourcery - **Trigger a new review:** Comment `@sourcery-ai review` on the pull request. - **Continue discussions:** Reply directly to Sourcery's review comments. - **Generate a GitHub issue from a review comment:** Ask Sourcery to create an issue from a review comment by replying to it. You can also reply to a review comment with `@sourcery-ai issue` to create an issue from it. - **Generate a pull request title:** Write `@sourcery-ai` anywhere in the pull request title to generate a title at any time. You can also comment `@sourcery-ai title` on the pull request to (re-)generate the title at any time. - **Generate a pull request summary:** Write `@sourcery-ai summary` anywhere in the pull request body to generate a PR summary at any time exactly where you want it. You can also comment `@sourcery-ai summary` on the pull request to (re-)generate the summary at any time. - **Generate reviewer's guide:** Comment `@sourcery-ai guide` on the pull request to (re-)generate the reviewer's guide at any time. - **Resolve all Sourcery comments:** Comment `@sourcery-ai resolve` on the pull request to resolve all Sourcery comments. Useful if you've already addressed all the comments and don't want to see them anymore. - **Dismiss all Sourcery reviews:** Comment `@sourcery-ai dismiss` on the pull request to dismiss all existing Sourcery reviews. Especially useful if you want to start fresh with a new review - don't forget to comment `@sourcery-ai review` to trigger a new review! #### Customizing Your Experience Access your [dashboard](https://app.sourcery.ai) to: - Enable or disable review features such as the Sourcery-generated pull request summary, the reviewer's guide, and others. - Change the review language. - Add, remove or edit custom review instructions. - Adjust other review settings. #### Getting Help - [Contact our support team](mailto:support@sourcery.ai) for questions or feedback. - Visit our [documentation](https://docs.sourcery.ai) for detailed guides and information. - Keep in touch with the Sourcery team by following us on [X/Twitter](https://x.com/SourceryAI), [LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/company/sourcery-ai/) or [GitHub](https://github.com/sourcery-ai).

Code Review Ô£à Approved 1 resolved / 1 findings Refactored SIMA fleet health checks to reduce cyclomatic complexity, resolving concerns regarding excessive allocations by removing unnecessary dictionary snapshots. Dispatch tracking logic was also adjusted to ensure metrics reflect only active processing cycles.
Ô£à 1 resolved
Ô£à Performance: ToArray() on ConcurrentDictionary adds allocations, not removes them > ­ƒôä src/V12_002.SIMA.Fleet.cs:536-537 ­ƒôä src/V12_002.SIMA.Fleet.cs:562 > The PR description claims "Replaced dictionary enumeration with snapshots (ToArray) and indexed loops to cut allocations", but this is backwards. The old code iterated `_followerBrackets` and `activePositions` (both `ConcurrentDictionary`) directly via `foreach`, which uses a struct enumerator with zero heap allocation. The new `HasActiveFsmForAccount` and `HasActivePositionForAccount` methods call `.ToArray()` on each call, which allocates a `KeyValuePair[]` on every health check invocation. > > The old code even had a comment: "ConcurrentDictionary is thread-safe for enumeration - no snapshot needed" ÔÇö the new code removes this comment and adds the snapshot anyway. > > For a method labeled as a perf improvement, this introduces per-call array allocations proportional to dictionary size. If these dictionaries are large or the health check is called frequently, this will increase GC pressure.

reviews.body:
This PR successfully refactors `ShouldSkipFleet_RunHealthCheck` to reduce cyclomatic complexity from 31ÔåÆ5 by extracting logic into well-defined helper methods. The refactoring maintains identical behavior while improving code maintainability and readability. All extracted methods are properly documented and follow the existing codebase patterns. No defects found that would block merge.

---
You can now have the agent implement changes and create commits directly on your pull request's source branch. Simply comment with /q followed by your request in natural language to ask the agent to make changes.

Hey - I've found 1 issue, and left some high level feedback:

- The new helpers `HasActiveFsmForAccount` and `HasActivePositionForAccount` call `ToArray()` on the underlying collections each time, which may introduce extra allocations compared to the original `foreach` on the concurrent dictionaries; consider keeping the lock-free enumeration pattern to preserve the zero-allocation AMAL requirement.
- Given that `ShouldSkipFleet_RunHealthCheck` already guarantees `acct != null` and `acct.Positions != null`, it would be helpful to document that `IsBrokerPositionFlat` relies on that precondition (or add defensive null checks) to make the helper safer if it is reused elsewhere later.

<details>
<summary>Prompt for AI Agents</summary>

~~~markdown
Please address the comments from this code review:

## Overall Comments
- The new helpers `HasActiveFsmForAccount` and `HasActivePositionForAccount` call `ToArray()` on the underlying collections each time, which may introduce extra allocations compared to the original `foreach` on the concurrent dictionaries; consider keeping the lock-free enumeration pattern to preserve the zero-allocation AMAL requirement.
- Given that `ShouldSkipFleet_RunHealthCheck` already guarantees `acct != null` and `acct.Positions != null`, it would be helpful to document that `IsBrokerPositionFlat` relies on that precondition (or add defensive null checks) to make the helper safer if it is reused elsewhere later.

## Individual Comments

### Comment 1
<location path="src/V12_002.SIMA.Fleet.cs" line_range="488-497" />
<code_context>
+                bool brokerFlat = IsBrokerPositionFlat(acct);
</code_context>
<issue_to_address>
**suggestion (performance):** Avoid recomputing dictionary snapshots for every account to reduce per-iteration overhead.

`HasActiveFsmForAccount` and `HasActivePositionForAccount` each call `ToArray()` on every invocation, so with `ShouldSkipFleet_RunHealthCheck` looping over accounts this becomes O(N_accounts * N_collection) in allocations and copies. Consider taking the snapshots once in the caller (e.g., `_followerBrackets.ToArray()`, `activePositions.ToArray()`) and passing them in, or inlining the loops to avoid repeated allocations and iteration per account.

Suggested implementation:

```csharp
                bool brokerFlat = IsBrokerPositionFlat(acct);
                bool hasActiveFsmForAcct = HasActiveFsmForAccount(acct.Name, followerBracketsSnapshot);
                bool hasActivePositionForAcct = HasActivePositionForAccount(acct.Name, activePositionsSnapshot);

```

To fully implement the optimization and avoid recomputing snapshots on every account iteration, you should also:

1. In the method that contains this snippet (likely `ShouldSkipFleet_RunHealthCheck`), **take the snapshots once before the loop over accounts**, e.g.:
   - `var followerBracketsSnapshot = _followerBrackets.ToArray();`
   - `var activePositionsSnapshot = activePositions.ToArray();` (or whichever collection is used inside `HasActivePositionForAccount`).
   These should be declared **outside** the `foreach`/`for` loop that iterates over `acct`.

2. Add/adjust overloads for the helper methods to accept the snapshots instead of computing them internally:
   - Change `HasActiveFsmForAccount(string accountName)` to `HasActiveFsmForAccount(string accountName, KeyValuePair<...,...>[] followerBracketsSnapshot)` (or the appropriate element type), and replace any internal `_followerBrackets.ToArray()` usage with iteration over the provided `followerBracketsSnapshot`.
   - Change `HasActivePositionForAccount(string accountName)` similarly to accept `activePositionsSnapshot` and use that instead of calling `ToArray()` internally.

3. If there are other callers of `HasActiveFsmForAccount` / `HasActivePositionForAccount`, either:
   - Update them to also pass in appropriate snapshots, or
   - Provide backward-compatible overloads that take only `accountName` and internally call the new overloads with a one-off snapshot (so existing call sites keep compiling while you progressively migrate them).

These changes together will ensure that the `ToArray()` snapshots are created only once per health-check run instead of once per account, removing the O(N_accounts * N_collection) allocation and copy overhead.
</issue_to_address>
~~~

</details>

***

### Pull Request Overview

While this PR successfully reduces the cyclomatic complexity of the main health check entry point and improves metric accuracy, it introduces two significant issues that should be addressed before merging. First, a performance regression in `IsBrokerPositionFlat` introduces heap allocations and O(N) complexity in a high-frequency path, contradicting the intent of the refactor. Second, while the main method complexity dropped, the extracted `LogHealthCheckResult` method now exceeds the complexity threshold (11 > 8).

Codacy analysis indicates the PR is 'Up to Standards' overall, but `src/V12_002.SIMA.Fleet.cs` remains a high-risk file due to its complexity combined with a total lack of test coverage for the new logic. The following feedback focuses on rectifying the allocation issues and cleaning up diagnostic accuracy.

#### About this PR
- The refactoring successfully simplified the primary loop, but the extracted method `LogHealthCheckResult` has inherited significant boolean complexity that makes it difficult to maintain.
- Despite the refactor to zero-allocation enumeration in other areas, the retention of `ToArray()` in the position checking logic poses a risk of GC pressure in high-frequency trading scenarios. Ensure all helper methods in this path adhere to the zero-allocation mandate.



#### Test suggestions
- [ ] Verify TrackSimaDispatch is not incremented when EnableSIMA is false or isFlattenRunning is true.
- [ ] Verify health check correctly identifies and logs accounts with active FSM states (Active, Accepted, Submitted, Replacing).
- [ ] Verify zero-allocation status for HasActiveFsmForAccount and HasActivePositionForAccount using an allocation profiler.
- [ ] Verify ShouldSkipFleet_RunHealthCheck handles null account or null positions gracefully without throwing.
- [ ] Implement unit tests for the extracted logic in `src/V12_002.SIMA.Fleet.cs` to mitigate risks in this uncovered complex file.
